docs.title: HKPUG PyPI 30-Day CTF
docs.text:
Learn PyPI by hacking a safe toy package ecosystem.

This site is the participant-facing tutorial and progress hub. The GitHub repo contains the raw challenge files; this MkDocs site explains how to play, how each lab works, and how progress is tracked.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
1. Read How To Play.
  2. Open the Tutorial Index.
  3. Start with Flag 01.
  4. Check the Scoreboard.

docs.title: Learning Promise
docs.text:
Every flag is a hacking lab:

  • point pip at a challenge index
  • inspect /simple/ pages
  • make pip choose a controlled package
  • inspect wheels and source distributions
  • trigger harmless local flag capture
  • explain the defensive fix

No real PyPI uploads, no real package names, no real credentials.

docs.title: MVP Trail
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  F01["01 Index Recon"] --> F02["02 Name Maze"]
  F02 --> F04["04 Dependency Confusion"]
  F04 --> F05["05 Wheel Autopsy"]
  F05 --> F07["07 Legacy Sdist Trap"]

docs.title: Participant Working Format
docs.text: This page explains how participants should work through the challenge.

docs.title: The Daily Loop
docs.text:
Each flag follows the same shape:

  1. read the scenario
  2. run the setup command
  3. inspect or modify the toy index/package
  4. make the controlled package capture the fake flag
  5. submit the flag and one short explanation
  6. optionally submit a defensive patch or writeup

The challenge is about learning PyPI by hacking a safe toy ecosystem.

docs.title: What "Hack" Means Here
docs.text:
Allowed:

  • point pip at the provided challenge index
  • build toy wheels or sdists inside this repo
  • edit challenge-owned index HTML files
  • run the victim app inside the challenge workspace
  • capture fake flags under artifacts/
  • inspect installed files in a temporary virtual environment

Not allowed:

  • uploading anything to real PyPI
  • targeting real package names
  • reading real credentials
  • contacting external callback servers
  • modifying files outside the challenge workspace

docs.title: Standard Lab Layout
docs.text:
Each lab should eventually follow this structure:

labs/flag-XX-name/
  README.md
  scenario/
  packages-src/
  indexes/
  victim/
  expected/

Some early tutorial labs may be lighter than this.

docs.title: Standard Flag Capture
docs.text:
The official fake flag is provided through a local environment variable:

export HKPUG_FAKE_FLAG="HKPUG{practice.flag-XX}"

The package or victim app should write proof to:

artifacts/flag-XX.txt

Example proof:

{
  "flag_id": "flag-04",
  "package": "hkpug-ctf-internal-utils",
  "version": "9.9.9",
  "source_index": "public-sim",
  "captured_flag": "HKPUG{practice.flag-04}"
}

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
For the MVP, participants submit:

  • flag value
  • one or two sentence explanation
  • optional patch or writeup

For hosted HKPUG mode, answers are encrypted before a pull request.

For community mode, participants can self-check locally and submit a public writeup after the official window closes.

docs.title: E-Cert Progress
docs.text:
Suggested thresholds:

  • Participation: 2 valid flags
  • Explorer: 5 valid flags including one resolver/build flag
  • Completion: 9 valid flags
  • Finisher: capstone plus defense checklist

docs.title: Scoreboard
docs.text:
This scoreboard is intentionally friendly. It should show progress and learning status before pure ranking.

The first version reads static data from:

assets/leaderboard.json

Later, the trusted scorer can update the same JSON file on the Pages branch. The same scorer can also apply hint multipliers.

Filter Loading scoreboard...

docs.title: Certificate Tiers
docs.text: Tier Suggested Requirement Participation 2 valid flags Explorer 5 valid flags including one resolver/build flag Completion 9 valid flags Finisher capstone plus defense checklist

docs.title: Hint Multipliers
docs.text:
For the MVP, hint state can be global per flag:

State Max Score Fresh 100% Hint 1 open 85% Hint 2 open 70% Guided open 50%

For a later private tracked hint system, the scorer can store per-team unlocks and apply the multiplier only to teams that requested hints.

docs.title: Lab Index
docs.text:
The first playable version should focus on 5 substantial flags. Each flag can span 2 to 3 days and contain several investigation steps.

Flag Lab Status 01 Index Recon stub 02 Name Maze stub 04 Dependency Confusion stub 05 Wheel Autopsy stub 07 Legacy Sdist Trap stub

Each lab should include:

  • scenario
  • objective
  • setup commands
  • expected artifact or output
  • hints
  • hint release schedule
  • defense notes

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are given a package that is not on real PyPI. Your first job is to map the challenge index, identify the project page, and install the selected artifact.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Make pip install hkpug-ctf-hello from the trusted challenge index and write proof under artifacts/flag-01.json.

docs.title: Working Notes
docs.text:
This lab should eventually point to:

labs/flag-01-custom-index-install/

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • install command used
  • selected artifact URL or path
  • one sentence explaining why real PyPI was not involved

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: Before using pip, inspect the index by hand. The project page is just HTML that links to package files.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Find the wheel URL and hash for hkpug-ctf-hello by reading the simple index pages.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• project page path
  • wheel filename
  • hash fragment or computed hash
  • practice flag

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The same package can be requested with different punctuation. Pip normalizes the name before looking up the project page.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Show that package name variants resolve to the same normalized project name, then find the correct project page.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• normalized name
  • package variants tested
  • project page path
  • practice flag

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The victim app trusts a private index but also uses a simulated public index. The same package name exists in both places.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Make pip choose the higher-version package from public-sim, prove which index won, and capture the fake flag locally.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • selected package version
  • which index won
  • defensive fix

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A wheel was installed. Before trusting it, inspect it.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Unzip the wheel, inspect the metadata files, and find the behavior that leads to the flag.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• metadata flag
  • distribution name
  • version
  • one suspicious or important file found

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The victim is forced to install from a source distribution. A legacy setup.py path writes a local marker during the controlled build.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Trigger the legacy source-build path and capture the fake flag under artifacts/.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• captured flag
  • whether pip used wheel or sdist
  • which build phase created the marker
  • one modern packaging mitigation

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You exploited an unsafe install configuration. Now fix it.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Patch the victim app so it no longer resolves internal packages from the simulated public index.

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
• patched command or config
  • explanation of why the old config was unsafe
  • practice flag from the fixed self-check

docs.title: MkDocs And Scoreboard Plan
docs.text:
Use the same repository:

  • main stores source docs, labs, and challenge assets
  • gh-pages stores the generated MkDocs site
  • GitHub Pages serves the tutorial site

This keeps the public working repo and participant tutorial site in sync.

docs.title: Scoreboard Model
docs.text:
The MkDocs page at scoreboard.md reads:

docs/assets/leaderboard.json

During development, this file contains demo data.

In hosted mode, the trusted scorer should update the generated Pages branch with a real assets/leaderboard.json or commit the source JSON and redeploy.

Recommended fields:

{
  "team_id": "hk-01",
  "display_name": "Team Name",
  "region": "Hong Kong",
  "flags": 7,
  "tracks": 3,
  "tier": "Explorer",
  "latest_flag": "flag-07",
  "score": 70,
  "certificate_eligible": true
}

docs.title: GitHub Pages Deployment
docs.text:
The workflow should:

  1. install MkDocs dependencies
  2. build the site
  3. publish the generated site to the gh-pages branch
  4. serve GitHub Pages from that branch

When the repo is ready to go public, enable Pages from the gh-pages branch in repository settings.

docs.title: Private Hint Design
docs.text:
The best private hint design is:

sequenceDiagram
  participant Team as Team Browser or CLI
  participant Pages as MkDocs Frontend
  participant GH as GitHub Action
  participant Ledger as hint_unlocks.json
  participant Score as Scorer

  Team->>Pages: choose flag and hint level
  Pages->>Team: create request payload with nonce
  Team->>Team: sign payload with team private key
  Team->>GH: submit payload + signature
  GH->>GH: verify team signature
  GH->>GH: encrypt hint to team's public certificate
  GH->>Ledger: record hint unlock
  GH-->>Team: publish encrypted hint artifact/file
  Score->>Ledger: apply hint multiplier when scoring

docs.title: Does This Make Sense?
docs.text:
Yes. It is a strong model because it separates responsibilities cleanly:

  • the frontend gives a nice user experience
  • the team signature proves who is asking
  • GitHub Actions is the trusted verifier and ledger writer
  • hints stay encrypted to the requesting team
  • the scorer can apply a per-team score penalty

The frontend should not be trusted to choose the correct hint or record the official "hint sent" mark. A public static frontend can be inspected and modified by anyone. It can prepare and submit a request, but the trusted action must verify, encrypt, and record.

docs.title: Request Payload
docs.text:
Example payload:

{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "team_id": "hk-01",
  "flag_id": "flag-04",
  "hint_level": 1,
  "nonce": "20260613T120000Z-7f4a2c",
  "requested_at": "2026-06-13T12:00:00Z"
}

The team signs the canonical JSON payload with its team private key.

docs.title: Unlock Ledger
docs.text:
The trusted workflow records:

{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "unlocks": [
    {
      "team_id": "hk-01",
      "flag_id": "flag-04",
      "hint_level": 1,
      "requested_at": "2026-06-13T12:00:00Z",
      "request_sha256": "..."
    }
  ]
}

The scorer reads this ledger. If hk-01 used Hint 1 for flag-04, only that team receives the Hint 1 multiplier for that flag.

docs.title: Hint Delivery
docs.text:
The action can publish encrypted hints under:

hints-unlocked/
  flag-04/
    hint-1/
      hk-01.cms

The file is safe to publish because only the team private key can decrypt it.

docs.title: Option A: Browser Signing
docs.text:
The MkDocs page asks the user to paste or load the team private key and signs in the browser with Web Crypto or a small JavaScript helper.

Pros:

  • nice UX
  • no local command line needed

Cons:

  • asking users to load private keys into a browser is uncomfortable
  • browser crypto and PEM formats can be fiddly
  • participants may distrust the page, reasonably

docs.title: Option B: Local CLI Signing
docs.text:
The page shows the request payload. The participant runs:

python tools/request_hint.py \
  --payload hint-request.json \
  --private-key /path/to/team_private_key.pem

Pros:

  • private key stays local
  • reuses the workshop signing model
  • easier to test

Cons:

  • less slick than browser-only UX

docs.title: Option C: GitHub Identity Only
docs.text:
Use GitHub login or comments as identity.

Pros:

  • easy for participants

Cons:

  • static GitHub Pages cannot safely hold OAuth secrets
  • GitHub comments are public
  • GitHub identity alone does not map cleanly to team certificate identity

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
For this project:

  1. MVP: public scheduled hints.
  2. HKPUG official run: encrypted scheduled hints if time is short.
  3. Better official run: signed private hint unlocks with local CLI signing.
  4. Later polish: browser signing after the CLI flow is stable.

The signed private hint model is absolutely viable with the current encrypted submission design. The main extra work is the request workflow and the hint_unlocks.json scoring integration.

docs.title: Repository Safety Model
docs.text: The challenge must remain hackable without letting participants hack the original repository.

docs.title: Threat Model
docs.text:
Participants control:

  • forks
  • pull request content
  • issue text
  • PR comments
  • signed hint request payloads
  • encrypted answer bundles
  • package code they run locally

Participants must not control:

  • main
  • workflow definitions
  • GitHub secrets
  • team allowlist
  • hidden answer keys
  • scoreboard updates for other teams
  • hint unlocks for other teams

docs.title: Trust Boundaries
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  P["Participant input<br/>issues, PRs, signed requests"] --> V["Validation workflow<br/>read and verify only"]
  V --> S["State branch<br/>scoreboard, hint_unlocks, encrypted hints"]
  M["main branch<br/>source of truth"] --> D["Deploy MkDocs"]
  D --> G["gh-pages<br/>generated site"]
  S --> G

  P -. must not write .-> M
  P -. must not change .-> D

docs.title: Branch Model
docs.text:
Branch Purpose Who/What Writes main source docs, lab source, workflows maintainers only gh-pages generated MkDocs site deploy workflow future scoreboard-state scoring and hint ledgers trusted scorer only

main should never be updated by participant-triggered workflows.

docs.title: Safe
docs.text:
• participant opens an issue with a signed hint request
  • workflow verifies the signature as data
  • workflow encrypts a hint to the team's public certificate
  • workflow writes encrypted hint output to a state path
  • scorer reads state and applies the team's hint penalty

docs.title: Unsafe
docs.text:
• workflow checks out PR code and runs it with contents: write
  • workflow parses issue text into a shell command
  • workflow commits to main after a participant-triggered event
  • frontend stores secret hints or keys in JavaScript
  • Pages site writes authoritative state directly

docs.title: Workflow Rules
docs.text:
Use these defaults:

permissions:
  contents: read

Only elevate in a specific job that needs it:

permissions:
  contents: write
  issues: write

Participant-triggered workflows should:

  • checkout trusted main, not PR code
  • copy only allowlisted files if PR files are needed
  • treat all submitted files as data
  • verify signatures before writing state
  • write only to state branches or encrypted output paths

docs.title: Pull Request Safety
docs.text:
For challenge submissions:

  • use pull_request_target only to inspect PR metadata and changed filenames
  • do not run submitted package code
  • copy only allowlisted encrypted files
  • verify manifest digest and signature
  • pass encrypted artifacts to trusted scoring

For community code contributions:

  • use normal pull requests
  • require human review before merge
  • do not expose secrets to PR workflows

docs.title: Hint Unlock Safety
docs.text:
The signed private hint flow should work like this:

sequenceDiagram
  participant Team
  participant Frontend
  participant Workflow
  participant State
  participant Scorer

  Team->>Frontend: choose flag and hint level
  Frontend->>Team: produce canonical payload
  Team->>Team: sign payload locally
  Team->>Workflow: submit payload and signature
  Workflow->>Workflow: verify signature against allowlist
  Workflow->>Workflow: encrypt hint to team cert
  Workflow->>State: append unlock record
  Scorer->>State: apply team-specific multiplier

Important details:

  • include a nonce in each request
  • reject reused nonces
  • reject requests for unknown teams
  • reject requests for hints not yet released, unless using per-team unlocks
  • never print plaintext hint text in workflow logs
  • publish only encrypted hint material

docs.title: Scoring Safety
docs.text:
The scorer should:

  • run only trusted scorer code from main
  • read participant answers as encrypted data
  • decrypt only inside the trusted job
  • never execute participant packages
  • write result JSON to a state branch or Pages artifact
  • avoid committing to source files

docs.title: Repository Settings Applied
docs.text:
Current hardening:

  • Actions default token permission: read-only
  • Actions allowlist: GitHub-owned, verified, and explicit approved patterns
  • main branch protection: no force-push, no deletion, linear history

Recommended next controls:

  • add required status checks once tests exist
  • require pull request review for main once the project has collaborators
  • pin third-party actions to full commit SHAs before the public competition
  • keep state writes out of main
  • use GitHub environments for any job that accesses high-value secrets

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GitHub Actions secure use: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/security/secure-use
  • GitHub GITHUB_TOKEN permissions: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/authenticate-with-github_token
  • GitHub protected branches: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches
  • GitHub workflow event security notes: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/events-that-trigger-workflows

docs.title: Tutorial Pages
docs.text:
The 20260530 workshop showed that participants need more guidance on the general working format. These tutorials should be short, practical, and linked from each lab.

Recommended reading order:

  1. 00-how-to-play.md
  2. 01-pip-index-basics.md
  3. 02-package-build-artifacts.md
  4. 03-legacy-vs-modern-packaging.md
  5. 04-submission-and-certificates.md

Each tutorial should answer three questions:

  • What am I looking at?
  • What command do I run?
  • What should I observe?

docs.title: How To Play
docs.text:
This challenge is a safe PyPI hacking trail.

You will:

  • create or inspect toy package indexes
  • make pip resolve packages from controlled indexes
  • inspect wheels and source distributions
  • trigger harmless local flag capture
  • explain the fix

You will not:

  • upload packages to real PyPI
  • attack real packages
  • use real credentials
  • exfiltrate anything

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text:
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt

Then open a lab:

cd labs/flag-01-custom-index-install

Read the lab README.md, run the setup command, and capture the fake flag.

docs.title: Where Flags Go
docs.text:
Most labs write proof under:

artifacts/

If a lab prints a flag instead, copy the printed value into your answer bundle.

docs.title: When You Are Stuck
docs.text:
Look for:

  • the pip install command
  • the --index-url and --extra-index-url values
  • the project page under /simple/<normalized-name>/
  • the selected version
  • whether pip chose a wheel or sdist

docs.title: Pip Index Basics
docs.text:
When you run pip install SomePackage, pip does not magically know where the package file is. It asks a package index.

The default index is PyPI, but in this challenge we point pip at local or hosted toy indexes.

docs.title: Important Options
docs.text:
Use one index:

python -m pip install --index-url file://$PWD/indexes/trusted/simple hkpug-ctf-hello

Add another index:

python -m pip install \
  --index-url file://$PWD/indexes/trusted/simple \
  --extra-index-url file://$PWD/indexes/public-sim/simple \
  hkpug-ctf-hello

The second form is where dependency confusion lessons start.

docs.title: Simple Index Shape
docs.text:
A minimal index looks like:

indexes/trusted/simple/
  index.html
  hkpug-ctf-hello/
    index.html
packages/
  hkpug_ctf_hello-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

The project page links to distribution files.

docs.title: Name Normalization
docs.text:
Package names are normalized for index lookup. Runs of ., _, and - become one -, and letters are lowercased.

Examples:

  • HKPUG_CTF.Hello
  • hkpug-ctf-hello
  • hkpug.ctf_hello

All normalize to:

hkpug-ctf-hello

docs.title: Package Build Artifacts
docs.text: Python packages commonly arrive as wheels or source distributions.

docs.title: Wheel
docs.text:
A wheel is a built artifact. It is a zip file with metadata.

Useful files:

  • METADATA
  • WHEEL
  • RECORD
  • package files

Inspect one:

python -m zipfile --list dist/example.whl
python -m zipfile --extract dist/example.whl /tmp/example-wheel

docs.title: Source Distribution
docs.text:
An sdist contains source that may need to be built before install.

That build step is the interesting part for several labs. Legacy setup.py source builds can run project build logic in ways participants should learn to recognize.

docs.title: Lab Question
docs.text:
For any artifact, ask:

  • Where did pip download it from?
  • Is it a wheel or sdist?
  • What version did pip select?
  • What code ran during build, install, import, or command execution?

docs.title: Legacy Setup.py Versus Modern Packaging
docs.text: This challenge includes old and modern packaging flows.

docs.title: Legacy Pattern
docs.text:
Older packages often use setup.py directly. This can mix:

  • metadata
  • dependency logic
  • build logic
  • arbitrary Python execution

That is why several labs ask you to compare wheel installs, sdist installs, and direct setup.py behavior.

docs.title: Modern Pattern
docs.text:
Modern packages usually declare build requirements in pyproject.toml:

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

Modern build frontends can inspect build requirements before building and can build in isolated environments.

docs.title: Security Lesson
docs.text:
Modern packaging is not magic. A malicious package can still run when imported or executed.

But modern packaging helps because:

  • build requirements are explicit
  • build isolation narrows accidental environment access
  • wheels avoid project build logic at install time
  • hashes and lockfiles help detect artifact substitution
  • trusted publishing avoids long-lived release tokens

docs.title: Submission And Certificates
docs.text: The hosted HKPUG mode should use encrypted answer submission. The community mode can use local self-checks.

docs.title: Official Submission
docs.text:
Participants submit:

  • encrypted answers
  • signed manifest
  • optional encrypted writeup

The trusted scorer decrypts answers, checks flags, updates the progress board, and records certificate eligibility.

docs.title: Community Submission
docs.text:
Community participants can:

  • solve locally
  • keep notes
  • publish writeups after the official window
  • use public practice flags

docs.title: Certificate Tiers
docs.text:
Suggested tiers:

  • Participation: 2 valid flags
  • Explorer: 5 valid flags including one resolver/build flag
  • Completion: 9 valid flags
  • Finisher: capstone plus defense checklist

Registration should collect a certificate display name separately from GitHub username.
